speaker: interviewer
text: We're coming to tape number 4. We're doing an interview of Bob Geminder. And this is September the 24th, 1995. And the interview is taking place in Palos Verdes, California, in Mr. Geminder's home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You and your brother are just finishing college. You came to California.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. I came to California. Actually went to Texas, joined the army, then came to California in 1958. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I was the first one out here. And Judy and I had met-- I don't know. Do you want to know how Judy and I met?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. By all means.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Judy and I met-- I was quite active in a fraternity at Carnegie Tech. So she was out with one of my fraternity brothers. And in those days-- of course, it's not true today-- those boys would drink too much. You know, the fraternity boys. So he got quite drunk. And I volunteered to take Judy home, because I was living at home and I had a car. This was in Pittsburgh. He was from out of town, living at the fraternity house. So that's how we met. Judy and I met basically because the date she was with got shickered.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we kind of dated off and on for about, uh, our relationship was like three and a half years. I left town. We dated a little bit when we came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then in 1959, we got engaged. It was around January, early part of '59. We got married on August 23rd, 1959 in Pittsburgh. And then the next day, we flew out to California. I had already been living out here. So this is where we settled. Not this house, but basically-- so that we've been married about-- well, just celebrated our 36th anniversary a few weeks ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mazel tov.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of work did you do when you got to California, a few of the jobs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was an engineer. And first I worked out at Edwards Air Force Base, telemetry engineer. Then I worked at Aerojet. I joined a little company called Mechanics Research. I worked for Hughes Tool for a while, the oil and gas magnate company. I then went on my own for about 11 years. And, presently, I'm working with a company called CINC, Costner Industries Nevada Corporation. And the Costner is Mr. Kevin Costner, sponsoring this firm. And we're building an oil-water separator we build and sell. So he he's sponsoring this company for the environment, to assist in the environment.

speaker: interviewer
text: A good endeavor.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it's been a engineering and sales and marketing kind of career that I've had up through today.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Now, how many children do we have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We have three, three children. First one was born about 31 years ago, because she's 31, Miriam, Miriam Lynn Geminder. Likes to be called Mindy. My second child is Ellen Yvette Geminder. And my son is-- and my second child is going to be 30 on October 14th, so in a couple of weeks. And my youngest is a son call Shia, S-H-I-A, Shia Geminder. And he's going to be-- he was just 26. One other thing I'd like to talk a little bit about is after-- roughly when I was about 20 or 21, I got, of course, very interested-- being a Holocaust survivor maybe this helped-- being interested in the family. So I decided to try to find family, in other words develop a family tree, if you will. This is way before this became fashionable, you might say, which was like 10 years ago. But, anyway, so wherever we went, Judy and I, wherever I went alone even, I would always look in phone books. And having an unusual name like that, whenever I found one, I knew that we were related. So I was able to relate how they're related to me and so on. And believe it or not, we found many, many, many Geminders. In fact, the half-uncle of mine in Israel, it's exactly how I found them. When I was in Israel in '68, I started looking through phone books and found him. And he led me to others.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at this point, we have family identified in Brazil and Argentina and Chile and Europe, of course, and Israel, and then all over this country, perhaps as many as 200. They all don't have the same last name, because some of them, of course, were ladies got married. But this kind of became a hobby with me. And I just wanted to mention it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one other thing I thought I would also mention, about four years ago, when communism finally disappeared in Poland, my mother and I decided to go back to reclaim our properties, because up to now these properties were-- you know, as you asked me earlier, they were Nazi-owned, then they were communist-owned. And now communism is gone and we don't know who-- just the city's running them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother when I went back, as I said, about four years ago. And we were very successful. We reclaimed-- at least we reclaimed the stuff that's standing. You know, there were a few bombed-out buildings that's just land. But we reclaimed the building in Bielsko. And we reclaimed the building in Katowice. So we own them. And the manager of these buildings, just to tie the whole-- see how the whole thing ties together, you remember Zutka, of course, Wujek's sister. Szutka's granddaughter-- Szutka unfortunately and her husband are dead now. But Szutka's granddaughter Kasha is running these properties.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's the manager of these properties, so she manages them for us, and of course makes a few dollars by doing that. At this point, it's kind of a break-even. The rents are so low there that unfortunately I'm not gaining anything from it. But I'm hoping my three children or their children will eventually have something that might be--

speaker: interviewer
text: Bob, in this whole discussion we've had and interview, is there anything special in your mind you would want to convey to your daughters and son and to your wife, and just to the world in general, about the circumstance of the wars?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let me just digress-- back off a little bit from your question, if you don't mind. I'll definitely come back to it. I do want to make one point. While my mother and I were in Poland-- that's four years ago when we went-- I went back and I was going to find this attic, because if I had to pick the worst time of my life during the war, it was obviously those three months, four months on the attic, where I was eating pig's food and raw eggs and so on. And I was determined to find this house and the Grukhalowa family. And through whatever means, it took a long time, I mean, a lot of driving, a lot of asking, we found it. In fact, unfortunately the lady was no longer alive. But her son was alive. And I went through the attic. It still looks the same. The hay was still there. And I found the son. And the son and I are in touch with each other. He was roughly my age. In fact, when he saw me, he ran upstairs and got a picture, the only picture I really have of me during the war, he and I on a sled during the war that he took. So, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] anyway, those were the bad times.

speaker: interviewee
text: Getting back to your question, you know, it's been asked many, many, times, why did I survive and how did I survive, how is it possible, and this and that. You know, I guess being an engineer, I think like an engineer or systems person. You know, if you think about digital technology, you know the bits, yes and no bits, you know? You have on and off switches, let's say. I think to survive something like any Holocaust survivor would probably tell you, you had to have all the switches be a certain way. They all have to be yes. You know, I have mentioned maybe a dozen incidents in this interview, but there were 100. There were 200. I don't know how many there were, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every second day or every day there was an incident, sometimes twice a day, which if something didn't turn out to be yes, I wouldn't be here. Or if it was a split second or five seconds' difference, I wouldn't be here. So call it luck, call it mazel, call it whatever. I guess all the switches had to be-- it all had to be yes in order for us to survive. And I-- you know, my parents, my mother and my stepfather, of course were extremely instrumental in this survival. They didn't eat when there was no food. We had something some of the time. And I don't know how they survived without food, because they would always give us their food. There was this strong, strong survive-- goal to have the children survive, the children. And we were of course the two children. It was so important for us to survive. And I can't say enough about my brother George, who unfortunately died of cancer in this country. But, you know, he had a good life until then, and not long enough. But he and I of course did all kinds of crazy things to survive, the bonding between us that made us survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe if I was alone, I wouldn't have survived without him also, because who knows? I may have gone crazy, you know, when you sit-- when you sit with someone for 14 hours a day alone someplace, you can sometimes make it better than alone. So it's these kind of things that I think were-- That's your memories.

speaker: interviewee
text: --that's--

speaker: interviewer
text: What message would you want to transfer on, though, to your children, to your families, to people in general?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know. I guess maybe this might be a good time to say this, though I wanted to say it last. I did want to-- the message to translate. It's things like this you know, Mr. Spielberg, that I do want to thank him, because it's just wonderful. You know, a month ago-- I think you know this, Bernie-- I had a heart attack, my first heart attack. I didn't need it, but I didn't have a choice. And fortunately, it was no big deal. Here I am.  I survived it. But one of the first things that went through my head when I was lying in the hospital was, oh my god, I didn't do the video. I knew it wasn't even scheduled at that point. I thought, oh my-- that became so important. So-- And when I got home from the hospital, you know, I got a phone call. I didn't even have to call. I got a phone call from the Shoah association--

speaker: interviewer
text: Foundation.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Foundation scheduling this. It's like they read-- they knew. It was time, you know? But so I do want to make sure and thank Mr. Spielberg for this kind of a thing, because it makes us feel so good when you say, pass it on. It makes us feel so good to make sure that-- I mean, I survived, but the ones that didn't, that it wasn't all for naught, you know, that there is something carried on that people will see it, people look at, people will remember. You know the worst thing that can happen is that my grandmother's memory should not be around. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, she saved my life several times, many other times. And she has to be remembered. You know, she died very young. She was killed at a very young age. She was my grandmother, but she was very young. And she can't be forgotten. And that's why this kind of a thing is just wonderful, that Mindy's children, Mindy's children's children, and Ellen's children's children and so on and Shia's will be able to see this and remember Golde Glotzer, for example, or my mother Bertl, because it's really important. In line with that, and I guess I'll show that a little later, but we put together a book that perhaps I'll talk about it later when I show the book--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. We have the pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: --on the Holocaust, for the same reasons. So I don't know if I answered your question, but that's kind of a roundabout answer.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's good.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my mother and father, my real father now, when they got married, at their marriage in 1931.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bertha, Bertl, and Mano Geminder. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a picture of my mother when she was a little girl with a cute little bow their hair, and her brother Artur, who unfortunately perished during the war, when they were little. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is a picture of my grandparents. This is Golde Glotzer, Ira Glotzer. And this happens to be my aunt Bella and her daughter Sylvia. My aunt Bella are the people we came to in McKeesport, Pennsylvania, right outside of Pittsburgh that we lived with. When I started the sixth grade, this is where I lived with this lady aunt Bella. And Sylvia's her daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: So these are my grandparents. This is the lady that saved my life so many times, and her husband Ira Glotzer. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is a picture of Szutka and Tadeus Kuzhminska. They are the ones-- this is again, to reiterate, the sister of Wujek, my stepfather's sister. They're the ones that we came to in Warsaw. She, of course, was Jewish and married Tadeus, who was not Jewish. This is their daughter Zasha. And her daughter, not shown in this picture, is Kasha, is the one that's managing our properties at the present time. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of my brother George, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of course when he was probably around 50 years old at that time, living in California. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of us, the family in the United States, when we all got our citizenship papers. Of course, my mother, my brother, Wujek, and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year would that have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: That would have been when I was-- 1947 plus five. 1952. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] This is a picture of Lola, Miriam, and her husband, present husband. Lola was the one that George was under her coat. And I was under Miriam's coat when we escaped from the Stanislawow ghetto. Lola lived to be 92. Miriam and Herman, of course, are still alive, living in Florida. Their last name is Tauber. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a kind of an affidavit, whatever one might want to call it, that I found during my family tree search. This actually shows the names. Like this is Jakob Geminder. That's my grandfather from my father's side, and his wife Malka. And then here are all the children, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6. And here number 5 is Mendel Geminder, born 3/5/1891. It says what he did for a living. And it talks a little bit about where he lived and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is like an official document produced in Poland showing the family. It doesn't show the two half-brothers or half-uncles of mine that survived the war, because this was obviously done before that time. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture taken in Germany in Aglasterhausen when we were at this UNRRA camp. And this is actually myself, George kind of looking away, Wujek, and my mother. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] My brother's first daughter, Hope, and her husband, Marc Levy, and their little daughter Lauren, or Hope's little daughter Lauren. Hope came to live with us when she was about 16. She was kind of a runaway child from a divorce from my brother, so she's like our extra child or our additional child to the family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Open this-- This is a book that we put together. of course, my mother's name-- In fact, this book was-- as you can see here, we dedicated it to George. And it was interviews by my wife Judy and her sister Susie, interviewed my mother for hours and hours and hours. And then we transcribed it and finally put it into a book form. We have this book in many of the museums, the Holocaust Museum in Washington, the museum here in Los Angeles, Yad Vesham. And it's essentially my mother's story as told to a couple of American girls, Judy, my wife, and her sister. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of our wedding, Judy and myself, when I really looked very, very young, August 23rd, 1959. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is a picture of little Tara, who's Hope's daughter. Again, Hope is George's first child, my brother George. He also had two other children with his second wife, Aggie Geminder. His other two children are Jennifer and Michael. And this Tara is Lauren's little sister, and Hope's daughter. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is again going back in time a little bit. This is my father. That's primarily what I wanted to show. My father and his sister Helen Rottersman are in this picture. But primarily, I wanted to focus this on my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are the other people, quickly, if you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [LAUGHS] that won't-- Again, this picture was taken probably in the late '20s, perhaps early '30s, like 1930. This is Egon-- this is Leon Geminder. He's the one that went to Brazil, their son Egon, and Margit. Margit just recently died. Egon is still alive, living in Rio de Janeiro. And this is Helen Rottersman, the mother of Joe Rottersman, my cousin who lives in Calabasas. Helen is the sister of my dad. This is a very recent picture, taken by our friend the Spillmans, like just a few months ago. Of course, me, Mindy, Miriam, my son Shia, Ellen, my wife Judy. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] Well, this is my wonderful family that have been my support through my life. My oldest child, Miriam, Mindy; my number two child, Ellen Yvette; and in absentia my son Shia couldn't make it. Of course, behind me is my wife, Judy.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to know if the family who've been listening to part of the interview and have lived with this courageous man in many ways have anything to say? Judy, would you have something to say to the family who may look at this years from now?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I've been married to this wonderful man for 36 years. And I think the fact that he is a Holocaust survivor has lent just an extra dimension to our married life. I never met a Holocaust survivor before I met him. I didn't know what his accent was. I was 16 when I met him. I didn't know what his accent was. And when he told me on one of our dates and told me some of the stories, I was just amazed, because I grew up in a very sheltered Jewish community in Pittsburgh. We knew about the Holocaust, but not much. And I was very young then too. But it's been a wonderful life with him and I'm very fortunate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And, Helen, what have you got to say? Mindy, excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's OK. I always like to be called Mindy, actually. Mindy reminds me more of who I'm named after, Mendel. And I guess I've always been very aware of the fact that my dad was a Holocaust survivor and how fortunate he was to stay alive and the family that did live through the Holocaust. And I've always thought it was just an absolute miracle, especially when over and over I heard more and more stories growing up about the horrors of the Holocaust, and especially my father's experience. And so I've-- and I realize that my father, every day he wants us to get up and live each day. And he's really brought a lot of life to our family. And I think it's no mistake. It's part of being a survivor. It's part of understanding that coming into this world was no-- it wasn't easy. And he always talks about how he didn't have much of a childhood, but it's never too late to have a childhood. And so I always felt like if I could live my childhood to its fullest, that maybe would help my father to relive the childhood. And we also shared a b'nai mitzah together. I shared my bat mitzvah with my father and we did the whole service when I was twelve and a half. And that was really exciting. And every family event that we've had-- every birthday, every celebration, every wedding-- the Holocaust comes up, and stories from the Holocaust, and usually bringing in all the miraculous, courageous things that my grandmother did. And so throughout our life we heard about these stories. But it wasn't until my father helped put this into a book form-- actually the whole family, we all helped type it in, and my mom interviewing and my grandma telling the story-- it never really made sense in a logical way. So it was really, really nice to have this book.  It still is. And I'm a Jewish educator. I teach children Judaica, Jewish culture, history, Jewish holidays, and also teach about the Holocaust. And I hope that through my joy and my celebration of being a Jew and also learning the old melodies and tunes and customs of the Jews from Poland and all those Jews that didn't survive, that somehow I can keep that culture alive, because I think it's just a horrible shame what happened during the Holocaust and how much was lost. And so I feel the only thing that I can do personally is to live my life fully as a Jew and to always remember my father's experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much. That was really nice. And, again, I know that we're probably coming to the end. I would like to-- I said it before and I'd like to thank again Mr. Spielberg and whoever else is making this possible, because it's just a wonderful thing. We thank you. We appreciate it. And Bernie, I want to thank you for your time. John, for your time.

speaker: interviewer
text: The pleasure's been mine.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's been great. And I'm so glad I have this-- will have this little memento that not only my children will have, but generations and generations, because if you think about it, I'm about 60. And when my generation is gone-- and I do hope to live until 120. But I'm probably the youngest Holocaust survivor, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, because I was just about four when it all started. So with me, it will end. But with these videos, it will never end. Thank you again.
